input_path: data\papers\Mertens and Denil - 2025 - Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: Lab‑scale gantry crane controlled by an Arduino-based controller. The physical twin comprises a steel gantry frame, two linear‑actuated axes driven by DC motors with gearboxes, an end‑effector gripper, and an embedded microcontroller. It operates in a small laboratory environment to carry a limited payload (typically < 2 kg) along a two‑axis track. The system is purposely lightweight and modular, making it suitable for experimentation, education, and demonstrations of digital‑twin concepts. Its accessible mechanical layout and open‑hardware controller allow easy integration of sensors, real‑time data acquisition, and bidirectional communication with a digital counterpart.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The digital twin delivers several key services that assist both users and the physical crane. These include:
• A trajectory‑generation service that optimizes the crane’s motion paths to satisfy kinematic constraints and reduce mechanical wear.
• A logging application that persistently records the crane’s state (motor positions, electromagnet status) for later analysis.
• A continuous‑validation service that compares real‑time sensor data against the kinematic model, flagging deviations that may indicate faults.
• A visualization dashboard that presents live telemetry, historical traces and model‑based predictions in an intuitive web interface.
All services communicate over an MQTT broker, making them accessible to researchers, educators and engineers interested in prototyping control strategies, validating models, or monitoring system health.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The exemplar relies exclusively on free, open‑source tooling. Each service—trajectory generator, logger, validator, and dashboard—is packaged as a Docker container and orchestrated with Docker Compose. MQTT messaging is handled by an Eclipse Mosquitto broker. Historical data are stored in a TimescaleDB time‑series database, while Grafana renders live and archived telemetry in a web dashboard. The digital geometric representation is supplied as a CAD model created in Autodesk Fusion 360, and the corresponding kinematic model is implemented in a Python module that interfaces with the sensor data stream. The entire codebase is publicly available under a permissive licence, enabling straightforward extension and reproducibility.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin architecture is realized as a containerized micro‑service stack that runs on a single personal computer. Three core services are deployed as Docker containers: a TimescaleDB database that stores continuous telemetry streams, an Eclipse Mosquitto MQTT broker that relays messages between the physical gantry crane and the twin services, and a Grafana dashboard container that visualises the real‑time state of the system.  The remaining functional components – a Trajectory Generator, a Simulation engine, a Continuous Validation module, and a Temperature Monitoring service – are implemented as Python modules bundled in a shared library called gantrylib.  These modules are executed as separate processes or threads within the twin’s runtime and communicate with the broker via MQTT topics.  The integration relies on a lightweight MQTT message bus; no external enterprise interfaces or interoperability standards are employed beyond the standard MQTT protocol.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The exemplar utilizes an InfluxDB time-series database to persist sensor data and ActiveMQ as a message broker for inter-component communication. Virtual machine images are supplied, preconfigured with recorded greenhouse data for replication and experimentation. No explicit computational model types (geometric, behavioral, analytical) or detailed model–data relationships are documented.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The Physical Crane Interface is a Python program that connects to the crane’s controllers and streams the crane state to the Logging Application, which continuously writes this state to a TimescaleDB database. Documentation does not specify the underlying transport protocol, the exact data types (e.g., positional coordinates, velocity, load), or the logging frequency beyond it being continuous. No explicit data quality or preprocessing steps are mentioned.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The Control Application is a Python application that consumes trajectories produced by the Trajectory Generator Service and issues control commands to the crane through the Physical Crane Interface. The command payload format, validation, authentication mechanisms, conflict resolution strategies, or execution‑confirmation protocols are not described in the given document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: An MQTT broker is deployed as the signaling backbone between the digital twin services (Trajectory Generator, Simulation, Continuous Validation, etc.). No further details are provided regarding the MQTT broker’s configuration, network topology, bandwidth or latency requirements, reliability or redundancy strategies, or security protocols such as TLS or authentication.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: All containers—including Physical Crane, its interface, the Control, Logging, and Digital Twin services, the TimescaleDB database, the Grafana dashboard, and the MQTT broker—appear to be hosted locally on the same premises as the physical crane. The document does not mention cloud or edge platforms, nor does it provide computational or storage requirements, scalability options, deployment models, or availability guarantees.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Services for optimal control are exposed from the twin via a REST API that feeds a scheduling engine. The twin’s simulation engine computes confidence bounds for future temperature trajectories; these bounds are visualised on a runtime dashboard. A Continuous Validation Service monitors real‑time sensor streams against threshold limits and triggers alerts to operators through WebSocket notifications. The twin also offers a prediction service that returns a probabilistic forecast (mean ± confidence interval) for greenhouse climate variables, enabling decision makers to weigh trade‑offs between energy consumption and plant growth.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil

block_1_judge.reasoning: The description accurately captures the lab-scale gantry crane system, mentioning its physical components (frame, motors, gripper, microcontroller) and its purpose for experimentation and education. It aligns well with the text which describes a 'lab-scale gantry crane' with 'Crane Frame', 'Motors', 'Electromagnet', and 'Arduino'. It correctly notes the system is modular and accessible. However, the extraction specifies 'DC motors with gearboxes', while the text refers to them as 'Stepper' motors (Fig. 4) and mentions 'Trinamic Motor Drivers'. The text also does not explicitly state the payload limit (<2kg), though it mentions moving 'small pucks'. Despite these minor technical inaccuracies, the description is highly relevant and specific to the system described. Quotes: 'physical gantry crane... consists of: The physical gantry crane including its controller', 'Crane Frame consisting of all the hardware constituting the physical structure', 'Two Motors and associated Controllers'.

The extraction accurately identifies the key services and their general functions, such as trajectory generation, logging, continuous validation, and visualization. It correctly notes the use of an MQTT broker. However, it includes minor unsupported details like 'reduce mechanical wear' and 'web interface', and omits some specific technical details found in the text (e.g., 'no residual swinging motion', 'distance metrics'). Quotes: 'Trajectory Generator Service: a service that utilizes a kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement trajectories', 'Logging Application: a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state', 'MQTT Broker: an MQTT broker for signaling between services'.

The extraction is highly specific and accurate, detailing all major tools and frameworks mentioned in the source. It correctly lists Docker, Docker Compose, Eclipse Mosquitto, TimescaleDB, Grafana, and Autodesk Fusion 360. It also accurately notes the open-source nature and the MIT license. Quotes: 'Database, MQTT Broker and Dashboard C4 Containers are containerized with Docker', 'Eclipse Mosquitto was used', 'CAD models of the crane made in Autodesk Fusion360', 'LICENSE file contains the MIT license'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

block_2_judge.reasoning: The characteristic is marked 'Not in Document'. The source text describes the general data flow: 'data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the twin’s services.' It mentions a 'Logging Application' that 'continuously logs the crane’s state' and a 'Continuous Validation Service' that compares measurements, but it does not provide specific synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, or temporal granularity.

The characteristic is marked 'Not in Document'. The text describes a single system consisting of one physical crane and one digital twin. It mentions 'The gantry crane system... consists of five containers' and 'The digital twin consists of six containers'. While future teaching extensions are envisioned (e.g., 'an entire harbor system'), the presented exemplar is a single instance without a discussion of multiple twin instances or decentralized architectures.

The description accurately details the architecture: a containerized stack (TimescaleDB, Eclipse Mosquitto, Grafana) and Python modules (Trajectory Generator, Simulation, Continuous Validation). However, the extraction lists a 'Temperature Monitoring service' which is not mentioned in the source text as a component; the source lists 'Trajectory Generator Service', 'Simulation Service', and 'Continuous Validation Service'. Due to this unsupported inclusion, the score is reduced. The description is supported by: 'The digital twin consists of six containers... Database... Dashboard... Trajectory Generator Service... Simulation Service... Continuous Validation Service... MQTT Broker' and 'For ease of deployment, the Database, MQTT Broker and Dashboard C4 Containers are containerized with Docker.'

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source text describes a self-contained system running on a single personal computer ('run all of the software on that computer') connected to a physical crane. It does not mention integration with external enterprise systems, interoperability standards with other systems, or data exchange with external parties.

block_3_judge.reasoning: The description incorrectly attributes the technologies of a *different* exemplar (GreenhouseDT) mentioned in the 'Related Work' section to the gantry crane system described in this paper. The GreenhouseDT uses 'InfluxDB database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines... with prerecorded data' (Section II.B). In contrast, the gantry crane uses 'TimescaleDB' (Section III.A), 'Eclipse Mosquitto' MQTT broker (Section III.B), and involves 'CAD models and kinematic model' (Abstract). The extraction is factually incorrect regarding the system under study.

The document explicitly describes the physical acting components. It lists 'Two Motors and associated Controllers, one to control the cart’s movement, one to control the hoisting movement' and 'An Electromagnet at the end of the hoist to pick up containers' (Section III.A). The component diagram (Figure 4) further details these as Stepper Motors and TMC4671-Eval controllers.

The document explicitly details the sensing infrastructure. It states the physical crane includes 'a Swing Angle Encoder measures the swinging motion of the hoist, and an Anemometer measures the wind speed' which are measured by an 'Arduino' (Section III.A). The 'Continuous Validation Service' also utilizes 'measurements of executed trajectories' (Section III.A).

The document outlines a specific validation method. It describes a 'Continuous Validation Service: a service that continuously compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics between them and compares the value with an acceptable threshold' (Section III.A). It also notes the use of 'upper... and lower... confidence bounds derived from the simulation' on the dashboard (Section IV).

block_4_docs_retrieved:
III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the Physical Crane Interface as a Python program connecting to the crane's controllers and the Logging Application continuously writing state to a TimescaleDB database. This is supported by the text: 'Physical Crane Interface: the interface to the physical crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the crane' and 'Logging Application: a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.' The extraction also accurately notes the absence of specific technical details like transport protocols and data types in the provided text.

The extraction accurately describes the Control Application's role in consuming trajectories and issuing commands through the Physical Crane Interface. This aligns with the text: 'Control Application: a Python application that uses the crane interface to control the crane.' The extraction correctly states that the document does not describe payload formats, validation, or conflict resolution, which matches the lack of such details in the source.

The extraction correctly identifies the MQTT broker as the signaling backbone for services. The source states: 'MQTT Broker: an MQTT broker for signaling between services.' The extraction accurately notes that further technical details like configuration, topology, and security are not provided in the document. This matches the evidence.

The extraction infers local hosting based on the text: 'plug in the physical crane to a personal computer, and run all of the software on that computer.' It correctly notes the absence of cloud or edge platform mentions. However, it conflates 'C4 containers' (abstractions) with 'Docker containers', stating that all components like the Physical Crane and Control Application are containers hosted locally. The text clarifies that only the 'Database, MQTT Broker and Dashboard C4 Containers are containerized with Docker', while the Physical Crane and its interface are Python applications (C4 containers). This terminology error slightly reduces the score.

block_5_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.

block_6_judge.reasoning: The document emphasizes that the exemplar is for 'open and reproducible science' and is 'publicly available' under an 'MIT license'. However, it does not contain information regarding data ownership policies specific to data governance, privacy protection measures, compliance with data protection regulations (such as GDPR), consent management, or data anonymization, which are central to the characteristic's definition.

The source text describes the architecture and implementation of the crane and its digital twin, mentioning services like continuous validation where 'operators are notified' if thresholds are breached. However, it does not describe cybersecurity protocols, access control mechanisms, authentication systems, threat mitigation strategies, or formal fail-safe mechanisms and risk assessments. The system is described as a 'lab-scale' setup without detailed safety-critical system considerations.
